title: Calling Your Representative Without Apologizing
dek: *You were taught that anger needs to be wrapped in politeness to be heard, but when you call your representative's office, that instinct to apologize dilutes the very boundary your body is asking you to defend. This is about learning to speak your position without collapsing, to hang up without softening, and to treat your own conviction as information worth delivering intact.*
bodyMarkdown:
TL;DR. You can call your elected representative without softening your message or apologizing for taking up their time. Your nervous system reads apology as submission, which drains the boundary-setting power your anger is trying to give you. Learning to deliver your position clearly and hang up transforms civic participation into a body-level practice of reclaiming your voice.

Someone told you that anger without an apology is aggression. That lie keeps millions of people from making the single phone call that could shift a vote, change a policy, or at minimum let a staffer know that real humans are paying attention. In our experience, the people most likely to skip calling their representatives are the ones who have spent a lifetime managing other people's comfort. They rehearse the call in their heads, add three layers of "I'm sorry to bother you," and then never dial because the whole script feels unbearable. But here's what actually happens when you call: a staffer picks up, asks for your zip code, listens to your position, thanks you, and hangs up. The entire exchange takes ninety seconds. There is no debate. There is no need to justify your feelings or demonstrate expertise. You are constituent data, and your call gets logged as a tally mark. The system is designed for volume, not for you to perform deference. ## The Physiology of Pre-Emptive Apology When you open a sentence with "I'm sorry to bother you," your vagus nerve registers submission. Karla McLaren points out in her work on emotional fluency that anger carries boundary-setting information, it tells you when something is wrong and gives you the energy to address it. But if you wrap that anger in apology before it even leaves your mouth, you short-circuit the very function it's trying to serve. Your body knows the difference. You can feel it in the throat, that tightening that happens when you swallow your position to make room for someone else's comfort. Over the years I've seen people practice their call scripts with "I'm sorry" three times in the first sentence, and by the time they get to the actual issue, their voice has gone thin and high. The anger that brought them to the phone in the first place has collapsed into pleading. This isn't about being rude. It's about recognizing that your nervous system reads apology as a signal that you are the problem, not the policy you're calling about. When you start with "I'm calling to urge Senator X to vote no on Bill Y," you anchor yourself in the information. When you start with "I'm sorry to bother you, I know you're busy, but I just wanted to say," you've already told your body that your position is an imposition. Gabor Maté writes extensively about how we learn to prioritize other people's emotional states over our own physiological signals, often as a survival strategy in childhood. That same pattern shows up in adulthood when you reach for the phone to call your representative and immediately start negotiating with an imagined staffer's annoyance. You are solving a problem that doesn't exist, and in doing so, you drain the clarity from your message. ## What the Staffer Actually Hears The person answering the phone is not personally invested in whether you apologize. They are tallying calls. They have a database or a spreadsheet, and your zip code determines whether your call gets counted. If you say, "I'm calling to ask the Senator to support H.R. 4321," they mark it down. If you say, "I'm so sorry to bother you, I know you must get so many calls, I just wanted to maybe suggest if it's not too much trouble that perhaps the Senator might consider possibly thinking about supporting H.R. 4321," they still mark it down the same way. Your apology does not make them like you more. It does not make your call more effective. It just makes the call longer and leaves you feeling like you performed deference for no reason. Across the articles we've published on this site, the single most common report from people who stop apologizing on these calls is relief. Not triumph, not power, just the quiet relief of saying the thing and hanging up. Staffers expect calls. That is the job. You are not interrupting a delicate operation. You are participating in the mechanism by which representatives gauge constituent priorities. When you treat your call as an imposition, you misunderstand the system. It is built for this. Your call is data, and data does not need to apologize for existing. ## A Script You Can Use Exactly As Written Here is a script with no apology in it: "Hi, my name is [your name], and I'm calling from [your city]. My zip code is [your zip code]. I'm calling to urge [Representative's name] to vote no on [bill number or issue]. This matters to me because [one sentence about why]. Thank you for logging my call." That's it. You do not need to add softeners. You do not need to acknowledge that they are busy. You do not need to thank them for their service before you state your position. You say the thing, and you hang up. If the staffer asks, "Is there anything else?" you can say, "No, that's all. Thank you." If they try to engage you in conversation or explain the representative's position, you can say, "I understand. I'm still asking them to vote no. Please log my call." You are not required to debate. You are not required to be convinced. You are delivering information. The first time you do this, your heart will pound. That is normal. Your body has been trained to smooth over every boundary with niceness, and now you are doing something different. Let your heart pound. Say the script anyway. Hang up. Notice that nothing bad happened. You did not get yelled at. You did not damage a relationship. You stated a position and ended the call. ## The Difference Between Clarity and Rudeness There is a fear that if you don't apologize, you will sound aggressive. But clarity is not aggression. Aggression is an attempt to dominate or harm. Clarity is the delivery of information without unnecessary cushioning. When you say, "I'm calling to urge the Senator to vote yes on this bill," you are being clear. When you say, "Vote yes or I'll make sure you lose your seat," you are being aggressive. The difference is obvious. Harriet Lerner's work on anger and relationships highlights how we are taught, especially those socialized as women, to fear our own clarity because it might be read as hostility. But the solution is not to pre-emptively soften everything you say. The solution is to practice saying things directly and let other people manage their own reactions. A staffer who is annoyed by constituent calls is in the wrong job. That is not your problem to solve. Your job is to communicate your position. Their job is to log it. If you spend your energy managing their hypothetical feelings, you are doing their emotional labor for them, and you are not doing your own civic work. When you remove the apology, what's left is respect. You respect the staffer's time by being concise. You respect your own position by stating it clearly. You respect the process by participating in it without performing a version of yourself that prioritizes their comfort over your message. That is not rudeness. That is boundaries. ## A Five-Minute Practice for Before You Call Sit down. Put your feet flat on the floor. Take three breaths that are longer on the exhale than the inhale. On the fourth breath, say out loud, "I have a right to call." On the fifth breath, say, "My position matters." On the sixth breath, say, "I do not need permission to speak." Write down your zip code, the representative's name, and the bill number or issue on a piece of paper in front of you. Write the one sentence about why this matters to you. Read it out loud twice. Notice if you added any softeners, "I just think" or "maybe" or "I'm not an expert, but." Cross those out. Read it again without them. Now pick up the phone and dial. When the staffer answers, read your script. Do not improvise. Do not add politeness. Just read what you wrote. When you hang up, put both hands on your chest and take one more long breath. You just did the thing. If your hands are shaking, let them shake. If you feel a little wobbly, that's your nervous system recalibrating. You just set a boundary without apologizing for it, and your body is processing that as new information. Some people find that a magnesium supplement like [Doctor's Best High Absorption Magnesium Glycinate, 240 Count](https://www.amazon.com/dp/B000BD0RT0?tag=spankyspinola-20) helps with the physical agitation that comes up after these kinds of calls, but the shaking itself is not a problem. It's just energy moving through. ## Why This Feels Harder Than It Should You were raised in a culture that treats anger, especially from certain bodies, as dangerous. Women are called shrill. People of color are called aggressive. Poor people are dismissed as loud. The message is clear: if you want to be heard, you must first prove that you are not a threat, and the way you prove that is by apologizing for taking up space. But that framework does not serve you when you are trying to influence policy. It just makes you smaller. According to the NIMH page on emotional regulation, learning to express emotions in ways that align with your values is a core component of mental health. When your value is civic participation, and your emotion is anger at an unjust policy, the aligned action is to call and state your position. Apologizing for that action creates dissonance, and dissonance wears you down. This is also why so many people avoid calling altogether. If the only way you know how to do it involves performing a version of yourself that feels fake and exhausting, of course you won't do it. But if you can call, say fifteen words, and hang up, it becomes possible. Not easy, necessarily, but possible. In my own practice, I have watched people go from "I could never call" to calling once a week, and the shift is not about confidence. It's about repetition. The first call is hard. The second call is still hard but familiar. By the tenth call, it's just a thing you do. You look up the number, you say the sentence, you hang up. Your nervous system stops treating it as a crisis. ## When You Want to Say More Sometimes the issue is personal. Sometimes you have a story that feels important to share. You are allowed to share it, but you do not have to. Staffers are logging calls, not conducting interviews. If you want to add one sentence about why this bill affects you directly, go ahead. "I'm calling to urge the Senator to vote yes on the healthcare bill because I have a pre-existing condition and I need coverage." That is enough. You do not need to explain your entire medical history. You do not need to prove that you deserve care. You do not need to make the staffer feel something. You are giving them a data point: constituent with pre-existing condition supports this bill. That is valuable information, and it does not require a narrative arc. If you find yourself wanting to tell a longer story, write it down first. See if you can get it to one sentence. If you can't, ask yourself what you are really trying to do. Are you trying to influence the vote, or are you trying to be seen? Both are valid, but they require different strategies. A phone call is for influence. A letter or an email or a town hall is for being seen. Know which one you are doing. Some people keep a small bottle of [RESCUE Bach Original Flower Remedies, Remedy®, Natural Stress Relief, 0.7 fl oz](https://www.amazon.com/dp/B01N9BEUCV?tag=spankyspinola-20) on hand for moments when the emotional intensity feels like it might spill over into the call itself. There is no shame in needing a tool to help you stay grounded. The point is to deliver your message, not to perform stoicism. ## The Accumulation of Calls That Do Not Apologize One call does not change a vote. But a hundred calls in a day make a staffer send a memo. A thousand calls in a week make a representative reconsider. The system works on volume, and your call is part of that volume. When you apologize, you do not subtract from the volume, but you do teach your own nervous system that your participation is a burden. When you stop apologizing, you teach yourself that you belong in this process. This is not about individual transformation. This is about the cumulative effect of people showing up without performing deference. Every person who calls and does not apologize makes it easier for the next person to do the same. You are not just influencing policy. You are participating in a shift in how people understand their own right to speak. You can also explore [more articles in this journal](/articles) that dig into the relationship between anger and boundary-setting, or visit [the assessments hub](/assessments) if you want to track how your relationship with anger changes over time. If you are looking for nervous system support during this kind of work, [our herbal cabinet](/herbs) has suggestions for plants that help with the physical agitation that often comes up. ## Frequently Asked Questions ### What if the staffer argues with me or tries to change my mind? You are not required to engage. You can say, "I understand the representative's position. I'm still asking them to vote no. Please log my call." If they continue, you can say, "Thank you for your time," and hang up. You do not owe them a debate. ### What if I start crying on the call? Cry. Say your sentence anyway. Your tears do not invalidate your position. If you need to pause, pause. If you need to hang up and call back, do that. But do not apologize for crying. Your body is responding to something that matters. That is information, not a failure. ### What if calling makes me so anxious that I can't do it? Start smaller. Call a local office instead of the main D.C. line. Call after hours and leave a voicemail. Send an email first to practice the script. Some people find that a GABA supplement like [NOW Foods - GABA with Vitamin B6 500 mg. - 200 Vegetarian Capsules](https://www.amazon.com/dp/B00B0YA0K0?tag=spankyspinola-20) helps take the edge off the anticipatory anxiety, but the real shift comes from doing it once and realizing you survived. You do not have to be calm to make the call. You just have to dial. ## What Happens After You Hang Up The call is over. You said the thing. Now you are standing in your kitchen or sitting at your desk, and your body is still humming with the energy it took to not apologize. Let it hum. Do not immediately distract yourself. Do not scroll. Do not eat something to push the feeling down. Just stand there for a minute and notice that you did it. This is the part nobody talks about. The call itself is ninety seconds, but the integration, the part where your nervous system updates its understanding of what you are capable of, that takes longer. You might feel shaky. You might feel proud. You might feel nothing at all and then cry an hour later in the grocery store. All of that is normal. You just practiced a new way of being in the world, and your body is adjusting. If you want to keep going, call again tomorrow. Call about a different issue. Call the same representative every week. Make it a practice, the same way you would practice a musical instrument or a language. Not because you are trying to become perfect at it, but because repetition builds capacity. The more you do it, the less your body treats it as a threat. And the less it feels like a threat, the more possible it becomes to show up exactly as you are, angry and clear and unapologetic, which is exactly what the system needs you to be. You can find more reflections on anger as a tool for clarity at [theoraclelover.com](https://theoraclelover.com), where the focus is on what happens when you stop treating your own intensity as a problem to solve. This work, the work of calling without apologizing, is part of that larger practice. It is small and specific and completely learnable, and it belongs to you. ---
Written by **The Oracle Lover**, independent essayist on anger, somatics, and embodied spirituality. Last updated May 29, 2026. In our work on calling your representative without apologizing, we keep returning to one quiet truth: the body has been telling you something for a long time, and it deserves a thoughtful listener. The Anger Practice is a companion journal to [theoraclelover.com](https://theoraclelover.com), and every piece here is written with you in mind.
